exercises.title: Video generator vs world model
exercises.prompt: Distinguish a pure video-generation model (Sora 2) from an action-conditioned world model (Genie 3, DreamerV3). What extra input does a world model take, and what does that enable?
exercises.hint: Video gen: prompt → video. World model: current state + action → next state/frame, so it's interactive/controllable.
exercises.solution:
- **Pure video generation (Sora 2):** input a text/image prompt, output a coherent video clip. It's a powerful *renderer* of plausible motion, but you can't steer it frame-by-frame — it's not interactive.
- **Action-conditioned world model (Genie 3, DreamerV3):** takes the **current state plus an action** ('move left', 'open door') and predicts the **next frame/state**, step by step. Conditioning on actions makes it an **interactive, controllable simulator** — a learned game engine / environment.

The extra input is the **action**, and it's what turns 'generate a video' into 'simulate what happens if I do X' — enabling agents to plan and act inside the model.

exercises.title: Anatomy of a video DiT
exercises.prompt: Describe how a video diffusion transformer represents and attends over a video: spatio-temporal patches, 3D position encoding, and joint attention across (T,H,W). Why is full joint attention expensive, and what's the common mitigation?
exercises.hint: Patchify across time+space into tokens; encode (t,y,x) position; attend over all tokens. Token count = T·H·W → quadratic cost. Mitigate with factorized/windowed attention.
exercises.solution:
A video DiT **patchifies across both space and time**: the video latent is cut into spatio-temporal patches, each becoming a token, with a **3D positional encoding** marking its (t, y, x) location. Transformer blocks then apply **joint attention across all (T,H,W) tokens**, so a token can attend to other positions *and* other timesteps — capturing motion, object permanence, and long-range temporal consistency.

**Why it's expensive:** attention is **quadratic in the number of tokens**, and tokens = T·H·W, so adding frames or resolution blows up cost fast (a few seconds of video is tens of thousands of tokens). 

**Common mitigation:** **factorized attention** — alternate spatial-only attention (within a frame) and temporal-only attention (across frames at the same location), or use windowed/sparse attention — which reduces the quadratic blowup while still propagating information across both axes over stacked layers. This is the same cost/structure tradeoff as image ViT vs Swin, applied to the time dimension.

exercises.title: World model in the robotics loop
exercises.prompt: Trace how a world model plugs into a robotics control loop (VLM plans → video model simulates → inverse dynamics emits actions), and explain the advantage over training the robot directly in the real world.
exercises.hint: The world model is a learned simulator: imagine candidate futures, score them, pick actions — without breaking real hardware. Inverse dynamics maps predicted frames back to the action that caused them.
exercises.solution:
**The loop:**
1. A **VLM (planner)** reads the goal + current observation and proposes a high-level plan / candidate actions.
2. The **world model** acts as a learned **simulator**: conditioned on the current state and a candidate action, it **predicts the resulting future frames** ('imagines' what happens). You can roll out and **score multiple candidate action sequences** internally.
3. An **inverse-dynamics model** maps a predicted state transition (frame_t → frame_{t+1}) back to the **low-level action** that would produce it, which the robot executes.
4. Observe the real result, repeat.

**Advantage over training directly in the real world:** real-world trial-and-error is **slow, expensive, and dangerous** — robots break, runs take wall-clock time, and exploration can damage hardware or surroundings. A world model lets the agent **imagine and evaluate many futures cheaply and safely in latent space**, learning from 'dreamed' rollouts (DreamerV3's core idea) and only acting in reality once a plan looks good. It's sample-efficiency and safety: practice in a learned simulator, act sparingly in the world. The catch is the **sim-to-real gap** — the world model's errors compound over long rollouts, so it's paired with short horizons and frequent real-world correction.
